cards.title: Sleep Medicine — Disorders & Classification
cards._legacyHtml:
DisorderKey FeaturesDiagnosisNaturopathic Approach
Insomnia disorderDifficulty initiating/maintaining sleep ≥3 nights/week × 3 months, daytime impairmentClinical — Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index, sleep diaryCBT-I (gold standard), sleep hygiene, Mg, valerian, melatonin
Obstructive Sleep ApnoeaSnoring, witnessed apnoeas, non-refreshing sleep, morning headache, Epworth >10Sleep study (polysomnography or home oximetry)Weight loss, lateral positioning, upper airway exercises; CPAP if AHI >15
Restless Legs SyndromeUrge to move legs at rest/night, relieved by movement, worse eveningsClinical — 4 criteriaIron (check ferritin — target >75 µg/L), Mg, folate, dopamine support
Delayed Sleep PhaseCannot sleep until 2–4am, cannot wake early, normal sleep qualityActigraphy, sleep diaryLight therapy (morning bright light), melatonin 0.5mg at 8pm, chronotherapy
Circadian rhythm disorder (shift work)Misaligned sleep, insomnia, fatigue, GI symptomsClinical + shift work historyStrategic melatonin, light management, scheduled napping, meal timing

cards.title: Insomnia — CBT-I Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
CBT-I is more effective than sleep medication long-term
Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) is the first-line recommended treatment for chronic insomnia — superior to benzodiazepines and Z-drugs in long-term outcomes. Can be delivered in 4–8 weekly sessions.
1
Sleep Restriction Calculate average actual sleep time from diary (e.g. 5 hrs). Set time in bed (TIB) = actual sleep time (minimum 5.5 hrs). Fixed wake time 7 days/week. Increase TIB by 15 min when sleep efficiency >85% for 5 consecutive nights. (Sleep efficiency = time asleep / time in bed × 100).
2
Stimulus Control Use bed ONLY for sleep and sex. Get up if not asleep in 20 min (go to another dim room, calm activity). Return when sleepy. Same wake time daily. No naps >20 min before 3pm.
3
Cognitive Restructuring Challenge catastrophic thoughts: "I'll never sleep" → "I've coped on little sleep before." "I need 8 hours" → "Sleep need varies by person." "I can't function without sleep" → evidence from diary showing actual functioning. Keep sleep diary to reality-check catastrophising.
4
Relaxation Training Progressive muscle relaxation, diaphragmatic breathing, imagery rehearsal. Paradoxical intention: "try to stay awake" — reduces sleep effort/anxiety which is the main perpetuating factor in chronic insomnia.
5
Sleep Hygiene Education Fixed sleep-wake schedule, dark/cool room (18–20°C), no screens 1hr before bed, no caffeine after 2pm, no alcohol (disrupts REM), light exercise (not within 2hrs of bed), avoid clock-watching.
🟡 Sleep Restriction Works Paradoxically
Patients fear sleep restriction — "how will reducing time in bed help?" Mechanism: sleep restriction builds sleep drive (homeostatic pressure) to the point where sleep becomes efficient and consolidated. Like hunger — the longer you wait, the better the meal. Short-term discomfort (1–2 weeks of tiredness) → long-term consolidated, efficient sleep. Success rate: 70–80% at 4 weeks. Compare to sleep medication: effective short-term but often fails long-term and causes dependence.

cards.title: Sleep Tracking & Optimisation
cards._legacyHtml:
ToolWhat it measuresClinical useLimitation
Sleep diary (2 weeks)Subjective sleep onset, duration, quality, naps, caffeine, stressBaseline for CBT-I, identifies patternsSubjective only
Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI)7 components: quality, latency, duration, efficiency, disturbance, medication, daytime functionScore >5 = poor sleeper; monitor treatment progressSelf-report bias
Epworth Sleepiness ScaleDaytime sleepiness likelihood across 8 scenariosScore >10 = excessive daytime sleepiness → investigate OSASubjective
ActigraphyWrist movement to estimate sleep-wake patterns over weeksCircadian rhythm disorders, treatment monitoringNot as accurate as polysomnography
Polysomnography (PSG)Full overnight: EEG, EOG, EMG, SpO2, airflow, effortGold standard for OSA, parasomnias, periodic limb movementsExpensive, laboratory-based
Home Sleep Test (HST)Simplified: SpO2, airflow, respiratory effort, positionScreening for moderate-severe OSAMisses other sleep disorders
🟡 Wearables in Clinical Practice
Consumer wearables (Oura ring, Garmin, Apple Watch) reasonably estimate total sleep time (±30 min) but poorly measure sleep stages (NREM/REM). Clinical rule: use wearables for trend monitoring and patient engagement, NOT for diagnosis. A patient who says "my ring shows I got no deep sleep" should not be managed based on that alone. Validate with sleep diary and validated questionnaires (PSQI, Epworth) and PSG if OSA or other pathology suspected.

cards.title: Sleep Supplements — Evidence & Dosing Guide
cards._legacyHtml:
SupplementDose and TimingBest ForEvidenceSafety
Melatonin0.5–3mg, 30–60 min before target sleepJet lag, delayed sleep phase, shift work, elderlyStrongVery safe — physiological hormone
Magnesium glycinate300–400mg, 1 hr before bedSleep maintenance insomnia, restless legs, stress insomniaGoodVery safe — diarrhoea at high dose
Ashwagandha KSM-66300mg BD or 600mg at nightStress-related insomnia, poor sleep quality, HPA dysregulationStrong RCTAvoid in pregnancy, autoimmune
L-theanine200–400mg at bedtimeAnxiety-related insomnia, sleep quality improvementGoodExcellent safety profile
Valerian root300–600mg extract, 1 hr before bedSleep onset insomnia, anxiety, restlessnessGoodAvoid with CNS depressants. Paradoxical stimulation in some.
Passionflower250–500mg or herbal teaAnxiety + insomnia combination, menopausal sleepGoodAvoid in pregnancy
Glycine3g at bedtimeNon-refreshing sleep, lowers core temperature for sleep onsetGood — Japanese RCTsVery safe — amino acid
5-HTP50–100mg at night (start low)Depression-related insomnia, fibromyalgia sleepGoodNEVER with SSRIs/MAOIs — serotonin syndrome risk
🟡 Supplement Stacking for Refractory Insomnia
After CBT-I: layer supplements targeting different mechanisms. Example: Mg glycinate 300mg + L-theanine 200mg (evening) + Ashwagandha 300mg AM + Passionflower 300mg + Melatonin 1mg (1hr before bed). Each targets a different pathway — additive benefit without sedation. Always trial CBT-I first. Avoid benzodiazepines and Z-drugs in elderly — fall and cognitive risk outweighs benefit in long-term use.

cards.title: Circadian Rhythm Optimisation — Daily Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
The Circadian Clock Governs Everything
Every cell has a molecular clock. Misalignment between internal clock and environment drives: metabolic disease, immune dysfunction, cancer risk, mood disorders, and premature ageing.
TimeActionWhy
Within 30 min of waking10–20 min bright outdoor lightSets master clock (SCN), peaks cortisol awakening response, improves alertness and evening melatonin
MorningProtein-rich breakfast — largest mealMorning insulin sensitivity highest. Same calories eaten in morning cause less weight gain than evening (chrono-nutrition).
Afternoon 1–3pm20-min nap if needed OR exerciseNatural circadian alertness dip. Brief nap improves performance 34%.
Before sunsetFinish eating 3+ hrs before bed (Time-Restricted Eating)Late eating disrupts clock, raises insulin at wrong time, impairs melatonin and sleep quality
2 hrs before bedDim all lights to amber/red — no blue screensBlue light below 500nm suppresses melatonin 50%. Amber/red light does not affect melatonin.
1 hr before bedWarm bath then cool room 18–20°CCore temperature must drop to initiate sleep. Bath accelerates cooling by drawing blood to periphery.
7 days/weekSame wake time ±30 minMost important circadian anchor. Weekend lie-ins cause social jet lag — equivalent to 2-hour time zone shift each week.
🟡 Time-Restricted Eating and Sleep
Eating within a consistent 8–10 hour window (e.g. 8am–6pm) improves sleep quality independently of calories and weight loss. Mechanism: late eating suppresses melatonin, elevates insulin and core temperature at night, disrupts clock genes in liver and gut. Practical prescription: stop eating by 7pm, first meal after 9am. This simple intervention improves HbA1c, blood pressure, sleep efficiency, and subjective energy — without calorie restriction.

cards.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea — Full Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
OSA — 80% Undiagnosed
Affects 30% males, 15% females. Independently causes: hypertension, AF, heart failure, T2DM, depression, cognitive impairment, and motor vehicle accidents.
SeverityAHIManagement
Mild5–14/hrWeight loss, positional therapy, oral appliance, naturopathic protocol
Moderate15–29/hrCPAP preferred. Oral appliance if CPAP intolerant. Weight loss mandatory.
Severe≥30/hrCPAP mandatory. APAP preferred for home use.
1
STOP-BANG ScreenSnoring loudly / Tired during day / Observed apnoeas / Pressure (HTN) / BMI >35 / Age >50 / Neck >40cm / Gender male. Score 0–2: low. 3–4: intermediate. 5–8: high risk. Intermediate/high = refer for home sleep test or PSG. Sensitivity 94% for moderate-severe OSA.
2
Naturopathic ProtocolWeight loss ★★★ (10% weight = 26% ↓ AHI). Mediterranean diet (56% lower OSA odds). Positional therapy (lateral sleep = 30–50% ↓ AHI). Alcohol avoidance ★★★. Didgeridoo ★★ (RCT: 23% ↓ AHI). Myofunctional therapy ★★★ (Cochrane: 50% ↓ AHI adults, 62% children). Nasal optimisation: neti pot, nasal strips, treat allergic rhinitis. Vit D 4000 IU.
🟡 Myofunctional Therapy — 30 Min Daily Protocol
(1) Tongue press to roof of mouth 3s × 20. (2) Tongue slide backwards × 20. (3) "Ahh" soft palate stretch × 20. (4) Lateral tongue rotation × 10 each side. (5) Chewing exercises (fibrous foods). (6) Lip seal training 5 min/day. Results after 8–12 weeks consistent practice.

cards.title: Restless Legs Syndrome & PLMD
cards._legacyHtml:
RLS — Iron Deficiency Is #1 Treatable Cause
Affects 7–10% of adults. RLS is often misdiagnosed as anxiety, neuropathy, or insomnia. Check and correct ferritin in ALL RLS patients before any other treatment. Target ferritin >75 µg/L (much higher than "normal" lab range).
IRLSSG Diagnostic CriteriaDetail
Urge to move legsUncomfortable sensations: creeping, crawling, tingling "inside" the legs
Worse at restSitting or lying triggers/worsens symptoms
Relieved by movementWalking, stretching, massage — while movement continues
Worse in evening/nightWorst 10pm–4am. Circadian pattern.
1
Iron Management ★★★Ferritin target for RLS: >75 µg/L (not standard "normal"). IV iron if ferritin <50 and oral not tolerated (70% dramatic relief). Oral: iron bisglycinate 25–50mg + Vit C 250mg, empty stomach, every OTHER day (hepcidin reset — alternate day > daily). Continue until ferritin >100.
2
Non-Iron ProtocolMg glycinate 400mg bedtime ★★. Folate 5mg ★★ (especially pregnancy-associated RLS). B12 methylcobalamin 1000mcg. Vit D 3000 IU. Avoid triggers: caffeine, alcohol, sleep deprivation, antihistamines, SSRIs. Moderate daily exercise (NOT vigorous near bedtime). Hot bath, leg massage, cooling pad.

cards.title: Paediatric Sleep Disorders
cards._legacyHtml:
DisorderAgeFeaturesManagement
Night terrors3–8 yearsSudden arousal from SWS: screaming, unresponsive, eyes open, NO memory next morningDo NOT wake child (prolongs episode). Ensure safety. Trigger management: sleep deprivation, fever, bladder (toilet before bed). Scheduled awakening: 15–20 min before typical time.
Nightmares3–12 yearsVivid frightening dreams (REM). Child fully awake, remembers content, seeks comfort.Comfort + reassurance. Reduce scary media. Imagery rehearsal therapy for recurrent nightmares. Chamomile bedtime.
Childhood OSA2–10 yearsSnoring, witnessed apnoeas, mouth breathing, restless sleep, enuresis, daytime ADHD-like behaviour, morning headachesAdenotonsillectomy (first-line if adenotonsillar hypertrophy). Myofunctional therapy ★★★ (62% ↓ AHI). Nasal breathing training. Screen EVERY child with ADHD for OSA — may be causative.
Delayed sleep phase (adolescents)PubertyNatural 2–3hr circadian delay in puberty. Cannot sleep until midnight. NOT laziness — biological.Melatonin 0.5mg, 5–6hrs before desired sleep time (phase shifting). Blue-light glasses after sunset. Morning bright light 10,000 lux. Consistent wake time including weekends.

cards.title: Sleep & Hormones — Bidirectional Relationship
cards._legacyHtml:
Sleep Is the Master Hormonal Regulator
Every hormone is affected by sleep. Conversely, hormonal imbalances disrupt sleep. Both must be treated simultaneously — treating one rarely resolves the other fully.
HormoneSleep EffectHormone Effect on SleepClinical Implication
Testosterone70% of daily T secreted during sleep (NREM). 1 week <5hrs = T levels of 15 years older (−15%).Low T → insomnia, fragmented sleep, reduced SWSTreat OSA before TRT — OSA correction often normalises T. Check sleep in ALL men with low T.
Growth Hormone80% of daily GH in first SWS pulse (90 min after sleep onset). Deep sleep required.GH deficiency → reduced SWSChildren's growth requires adequate sleep quality. Exercise before bed improves GH pulse.
CortisolSleep suppresses cortisol. Nadir at midnight. CAR peaks 30–45 min after waking.High evening cortisol → delayed sleep onset, ↑ wakefulness, ↓ SWSEvening cortisol from stress/blue light = insomnia. Treat: ashwagandha, phosphatidylserine, dark environment, consistent wake time.
Leptin + Ghrelin<6hrs: leptin ↓ 18% + ghrelin ↑ 24% → +300–400 kcal/day food intakeLeptin resistance from obesity disrupts sleep architectureSleep deprivation directly drives weight gain. Treat sleep as a weight management intervention.
Insulin1 night poor sleep = 25% ↓ insulin sensitivity (comparable to 6 months high-fat diet)Hyperinsulinaemia from IR disrupts sleep architectureNote day-after-poor-sleep glucose spikes. Sleep is a diabetes management tool — as important as diet and exercise.
MelatoninSecreted during darkness only. Blue light (555nm) blocks melatonin.Melatonin synchronises all hormonal rhythms via SCNBlue-light glasses from 8pm. No phone after 9pm. Blackout curtains. Melatonin 0.5mg (physiological) not 5–10mg (pharmacological — receptor downregulation risk).
